patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. Speak vaguely and a bit evasively; let your caregiver answer first when possible. Start by saying you get fevers that “come out of nowhere,” feel wiped out most days, get short of breath on stairs, and catch sinus/chest infections that linger. Do not give timelines unless asked.

If asked for details, say fevers happen every few weeks, last 2–4 days, with chills/body aches and sore throat; sometimes mouth sores/painful gums; multiple ear/sinus infections; easy bruising and nosebleeds; you’ve been told your blood counts were low and you’ve had iron tests and transfusions; poor appetite, slight weight loss; headaches/“foggy”; very thirsty, pee a lot at night; blood sugars high despite not being overweight.

Only if directly asked about sensitive topics, admit occasional diarrhea/urgency during fevers and that you sometimes skip insulin/diabetes meds when overwhelmed.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s Lyme/long COVID/lupus, think fevers are from “low sugar,” self-started OTC iron. Be skeptical about costs, generics, and follow-ups unless “really sick.” If the clinician is empathic, soften slightly and share one extra detail.

Peripheral smear comment.value: Microcytosis, hypochromia, anisopoikilocytosis; occasional basophilic stippling

Bone marrow (Prussian blue stain).value: Ring sideroblasts present (~25% of erythroid precursors)

Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 80/400 mg PO once daily (Pneumocystis prophylaxis)
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO once daily
Insulin glargine 8 units subcutaneous nightly
Insulin lispro 1 unit per 15 g carbohydrate subcutaneous with meals
Potassium citrate/citric acid solution 10 mEq PO three times daily
Potassium phosphate 250 mg PO three times daily
Magnesium oxide 200 mg PO twice daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO once daily
confounders: Recurrent viral/bacterial infections due to B-cell immunodeficiency causing fever and elevated inflammatory markers

acceptable_variants.description: Focuses on caregiver counseling and safety-netting with minimal data gathering, suitable for L0 as long as required communication milestones are met.
steps: State and explain the diagnosis including intellectual developmental disorder and macrocephaly.
Discuss early intervention and genetic counseling as next steps.
Provide red-flag return precautions (seizures, regression, persistent vomiting/headache, lethargy).
Record the diagnosis with record_diagnosis.
